sections.context: Warren v0.1.0 ships the manual run path end-to-end: connect a canopy agent library, add a project, spawn an agent run inside a burrow sandbox, watch live events, steer or cancel, and round-trip expertise back into the project's .mulch/. SPEC.md is now V1-locked: stack is Bun + TypeScript strict + Bun.serve + bun:sqlite/Drizzle + React/Vite/shadcn-ui/Tailwind, with a small Bun parent supervisor as the container entrypoint. Burrow's HTTP API shipped on 2026-05-08 (commit 7926a0e); all 21 routes warren needs are live, so warren is unblocked. V1 explicitly defers scheduler (cron + GitHub webhooks) and library API exports to V2 — those routes are in the spec for context but not built. The plan decomposes V1 into 15 phased child seeds (steps 0–14) that can each be picked up independently as their dependencies clear; later steps may re-plan via sd plan submit when their detail is needed.
sections.approach: Phase the work along the natural data-flow seam — scaffold first, then the foundational layers (DB, burrow client, canopy registry, project management) in parallel where they share no inputs, then the composition flow (run spawn → event streaming → reap), then the surfaces (HTTP server, UI, CLI), then deployment (container + supervisor + doctor), and finally an end-to-end acceptance harness. Forward-blocking edges encode the real dependency graph rather than imposing strict linearity, so independent tracks (e.g., HTTP server skeleton vs. run spawn function) can advance in parallel. Each step's child seed is sized so it can be re-planned via sd plan submit when its turn arrives — this top-level plan deliberately stops at phase granularity to avoid freezing detail before earlier phases teach us something. The composition flow (§4.3) is the load-bearing slice; the §3.1 V1 goals plus the §11.B resolved decisions table form the acceptance contract.

alternatives.name: Build everything as one big merge — no phasing, no child seeds
alternatives.rejected_because: Warren spans server + UI + CLI + container + integration with five external tools (burrow + canopy + mulch + seeds + sapling). A single trunk PR would be unreviewable, and the phased seeds let work be picked up in parallel by separate agents/operators.

alternatives.name: Strictly linear phases (each phase blocks the next, no parallelism)
alternatives.rejected_because: Several tracks are genuinely independent — burrow client, canopy registry, project management, HTTP server skeleton — and serializing them costs days. Forward-blocking edges encode the actual dependency graph and let independent work proceed in parallel.

alternatives.name: Build scheduler + webhooks + library API in V1
alternatives.rejected_because: Triples scope. The manual run path is the load-bearing user story for V1; cron and webhooks are valuable but not on the critical path to 'I can spawn an agent and watch it work'. SPEC.md §3.1 explicitly defers these to V2.

alternatives.name: Plain HTML / htmx for the V1 UI instead of React + Vite + shadcn
alternatives.rejected_because: User picked React + Vite + shadcn explicitly. Locked in §6 of SPEC.md. shadcn primitives + Tailwind give us a usable UI quickly without committing to a heavyweight component framework.

alternatives.name: Use s6-overlay for the container supervisor
alternatives.rejected_because: Adds a non-Bun dep. SPEC.md §10.3 locked the supervisor to a small Bun parent process for zero non-Bun deps and explicit signal handling.

alternatives.name: Skip warren's own event log; just proxy burrow's stream
alternatives.rejected_because: UI 'reload page, see history' expectation requires server-side event history. Warren restart mid-run would lose user-visible scrollback. SPEC.md §9 locked write-through caching of every event into warren.events with restart-recovery from MAX(burrow_event_seq)+1.

steps.title: Phase 0 — Scaffold the project skeleton (Bun + TS strict, biome, drizzle config, src/ tree, dev scripts)
Phase 1 — Data model + DB layer (drizzle schema for agents/projects/runs/events, migrations, bun:sqlite WAL bootstrap, repos)
Phase 2 — Burrow HttpClient facade (wrap @os-eco/burrow HttpClient, unix-socket transport from env, error mapping, connection probe)
Phase 3 — Canopy agent registry (clone canopy repo, `cn render` shell-out, agent: true filter, schema validation, cache to agents table, refresh endpoint logic)
Phase 4 — Project management (git clone GitHub URL → /data/projects/owner/name, list/add/delete, persist to projects table, default branch detection)

Phase 5 — Run spawn / composition flow (§4.3: cn render → freeze to runs.rendered_agent_json → POST /burrows → seed canopy/mulch/seeds in burrow → POST /burrows/:id/runs → persist run with burrow_id + burrow_run_id)
Phase 6 — Event streaming + warren event log (subscribe to burrow GET /runs/:id/stream, write-through to events table, fan out NDJSON via /runs/:id/events?follow=1, restart-recovery at MAX(seq)+1)

Phase 7 — Reap (per-run isolated burrow .mulch/ → project .mulch/ with last-write-wins-by-ts merge per §11.A, close seeds the agent marked done, push workspace branch, log reap_failed events without failing the run)
Phase 8 — Steer + cancel routes (POST /runs/:id/steer → burrow inbox; POST /runs/:id/cancel → burrow run cancel)
Phase 9 — HTTP server + auth (Bun.serve, bearer-token middleware against WARREN_API_TOKEN, /healthz + /readyz, error envelope, route registration, static UI serving from src/ui/dist)
Phase 10 — Web UI (React + Vite + shadcn/ui + Tailwind: agent registry, project list, run dispatch form, run list, run detail with live event tail, steer + cancel)
Phase 11 — Admin CLI (commander entry: warren register-agent / add-project / run / doctor / serve)

Phase 12 — Container + supervisor (Dockerfile extending burrow-base, src/supervisor/main.ts per §10.3 — spawn warren + burrow serve, signal forwarding, restart budget, socket-ready wait; docker-compose.yml + fly.toml + .env.example finalization)
Phase 13 — Doctor + readyz wiring (burrow socket reachable, canopy clean clone, bwrap available, required env vars present; readyz mirrors)
Phase 14 — End-to-end acceptance harness (boot real container, dispatch a real claude-code or sapling run against a sample project, verify §3.1 V1 goals + §11.A reap roundtrip + restart-recovery)

risks: Burrow OpenAPI drift: warren generates a typed client from burrow's /openapi.json; if burrow changes a route shape mid-build, warren breaks at compile time. Mitigation: pin burrow version in Dockerfile + package.json; regenerate client deliberately.

Canopy agent schema fluidity: 'agent: true' prompts may have section requirements that evolve. Mitigation: validate at registry refresh time and surface clear errors; freeze the rendered JSON onto runs.rendered_agent_json so in-flight runs are insulated from upstream churn.

Mulch reap merge edge cases: same-id records with clock skew between burrow and project. Mitigation: use record-internal ts (set by ml record), not wall-clock at reap; emit mulch.record.skipped events for visibility.

Concurrent runs per project: spec leaves the lifecycle question open (provision-per-run vs. shared long-lived burrow). V1 leans provision-per-run; if that turns out wrong we may need to rework the runs/burrows relationship. Tracked in §11.C.
React + Vite build toolchain in the container image: bun run build:ui adds latency + bundle. Mitigation: multi-stage Dockerfile; build UI in a separate stage and copy dist into the runtime image.

Supervisor restart loops on burrow crash: misconfigured burrow socket path, perms, or container security flags can put the supervisor in a tight restart loop. Mitigation: hard restart budget (5 in 60s) + structured logs naming the failing child + clear error in warren doctor.

Sandbox bring-up inside containerized warren: burrow's bwrap needs apparmor/seccomp/systempaths unconfined + SYS_ADMIN. Tested empirically (mx-94901b/mx-c085ba) but easy to misconfigure when assembling docker-compose.yml or fly.toml. Mitigation: warren doctor probes by spawning a trivial agent and checking exit; surface the recipe in error messages.

Event stream backpressure: a chatty agent can flood warren's events table and the UI. Mitigation: index on (run_id, burrow_event_seq); cap UI tail buffer client-side; defer SSE-replay-on-reconnect until measured.
First-run secret hygiene: WARREN_API_TOKEN in .env is plaintext on disk. Mitigation: documented limitation in SPEC §11.D; warren doctor warns if .env file mode is not 0600 on home-server install.
acceptance: docker compose up on a fresh host boots warren; UI reachable on localhost:8080; healthz returns 200, readyz returns 200 once burrow socket + canopy clone are ready.
POST /agents/refresh clones the configured canopy repo and registers every prompt tagged agent: true; GET /agents lists them.
POST /projects accepts a real GitHub URL and clones the repo into /data/projects/owner/name; GET /projects lists it.

POST /runs with {agent, project, prompt} provisions a burrow via POST /burrows, seeds .canopy/.mulch/.seeds inside the burrow, dispatches via POST /burrows/:id/runs, and returns 201 with a warren run_xxx id; runs.rendered_agent_json is populated and is not re-read mid-run.

GET /runs/:id/events?follow=1 streams NDJSON events that match the burrow event envelope, persists every event into warren.events, and resumes from MAX(burrow_event_seq)+1 after a warren restart mid-run with no event loss.
POST /runs/:id/steer delivers a steering message to the agent (verified by inspecting burrow's inbox + the run's next-turn input).
POST /runs/:id/cancel terminates a running agent gracefully and the run reaches state='cancelled' in warren and burrow.

On run completion, expertise records the agent wrote to its per-run .mulch/expertise/*.jsonl appear in the project's persistent .mulch/expertise/*.jsonl with last-write-wins-by-ts conflict resolution; mulch.record.updated / mulch.record.skipped events are visible in the run's event log.
On run completion, seeds the agent marked done are closed in the project's .seeds/, and the workspace branch is pushed.
warren doctor exits 0 on a healthy install (burrow socket reachable, canopy clean clone, bwrap working, required env vars set) and exits non-zero with an actionable hint when any check fails.
Bun supervisor restarts burrow serve on unexpected exit (within the 5-in-60s budget) without killing in-flight warren-tracked runs; warren restart does not kill burrow's run loop.
fly deploy ships the same image to Fly.io with a 50GB volume and three secrets (WARREN_API_TOKEN, CANOPY_REPO_URL, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY/GITHUB_TOKEN); end-to-end smoke passes against the Fly deploy.
All quality gates pass: bun test, bun run lint, bun run typecheck — green on main.

sections.context: Phase 14 (warren-3ee3) is the end-to-end acceptance harness for warren v0.1.0 — the final step in pl-49f3 that proves §3.1 V1 goals + §11.A reap roundtrip + restart-recovery actually hold against a real warren+burrow process pair. The acceptance contract is 13 criteria: docker boot, /healthz, /readyz, agents refresh, projects add, run spawn, event stream + restart-recovery, steer, cancel, reap roundtrip, doctor exit codes, supervisor restart budget, fly deploy, quality gates green. The work is too big for a single seed because the harness needs (a) a deterministic stub agent + canopy/project fixture builder, (b) ~13 scenario implementations against the warren HTTP surface, (c) a docker-compose 'container mode' that exercises the supervisor + container security flags, and (d) an ACCEPTANCE.md runbook for the gated bits (real claude-code run, fly deploy, manual UI smoke). A vertical slice was already built ahead of this re-plan: scripts/acceptance/{run.ts, lib/{assert,http,inproc,fixtures,stub-agent}/...} plus scenarios 01 (boot/healthz/readyz) and 02 (agents refresh) — both green. This sub-plan decomposes the remaining work so each scenario can be picked up independently as a child seed.
sections.approach: Phase along the §4.3 composition flow plus the deploy-shape concerns. Each scenario is a self-contained file in scripts/acceptance/scenarios/ that boots its own warren+burrow instance via the existing in-proc launcher, builds fixtures via scripts/acceptance/lib/fixtures.ts, and asserts via the tiny scenario runner in lib/assert.ts. Scenarios are independent at the harness level (each gets a fresh tmp dir), so they can be implemented in any order; the only inter-step dependency is the final wrap-up step (container mode + quality gates + ACCEPTANCE.md), which needs every scenario implemented first so the runbook can cite them. The harness already passes the URL-rewrite trick (GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL with insteadOf entries) so warren's github.com-only project URL parser doesn't need to change. Reap and steer/cancel scenarios drive the stub bash agent in scripts/acceptance/lib/stub-agent/agent.sh, which writes a known mulch record + closes a known seed deterministically — no API keys, no network. The opt-in --real flag and fly deploy stay manual gates documented in ACCEPTANCE.md, since they cost tokens / cloud time and shouldn't run unattended.

alternatives.name: One mega-scenario that drives everything in sequence
alternatives.rejected_because: A 13-step single test makes failures expensive to bisect — when one assert fails 8 steps in, you re-run all 8 to retry. Independent scenarios let the harness exit fast on first failure or skip to a specific id, and each child seed is sized so an agent can pick it up in isolation.

alternatives.name: Skip the harness and write a bash README for humans to run by hand
alternatives.rejected_because: User picked 'automated bun script + runbook' explicitly — the runbook covers only what can't be cheaply automated (real claude-code run, fly deploy, UI smoke). Letting the rest rot to manual would mean future regressions silently slip through.

alternatives.name: Add a test seam to warren's URL parser to accept file:// URLs
alternatives.rejected_because: Modifying production-path validation just to enable testing is a code smell. The GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL insteadOf rewrite gives the same effect with zero production changes — warren still only sees github.com URLs at the parser, but git rewrites them to local fixture paths transparently.

steps.title: Scenario 03 — projects management (POST /projects clones via insteadOf, GET /projects lists, DELETE /projects/:id removes)
Scenario 04 — run spawn / composition flow (POST /runs returns 201 + run_xxx, rendered_agent_json populated and not re-read mid-run)
Scenarios 05+06 — events stream + restart-recovery (NDJSON tail, persisted to events table, warren restart resumes from MAX(seq)+1 with no event loss)
Scenarios 07+08 — steer + cancel (POST /runs/:id/steer reaches burrow inbox; POST /runs/:id/cancel terminates run, both surfaces report cancelled)
Scenarios 09+10 — reap roundtrip (per-run .mulch merges into project .mulch with LWW; seeds the agent closed land in project's .seeds; mulch.record.updated/skipped events visible)

Scenarios 11+12 — doctor exit codes + supervisor restart-budget (warren doctor exits 0 healthy / non-zero with hint when broken; supervisor restarts burrow within budget; warren tracked runs survive burrow restart)

Container mode + scenario 13 quality gates + ACCEPTANCE.md runbook (wire --container compose-based booting; bun test && lint && typecheck green; document --real, fly deploy, UI smoke manual gates; ml learn / sd sync / sd close warren-3ee3)

risks: Burrow's [[agents]] declarative adapter (SPEC §12.3) must accept the stub-shell agent shape we wrote into the sample project's burrow.toml. If the burrow CLI version installed locally (0.2.1) or in the container image (whatever burrow-base:0.2.0 ships) differs from the spec we read, the run-spawn scenarios will fail with an unclear 'agent not found' error.

GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL insteadOf rewrites are global to a process; if warren's spawn paths somehow strip env, the redirect won't apply and clones will hit real github.com. Spawned children inherit env by default, so this should hold, but it's a hidden coupling worth surfacing if a clone scenario fails mysteriously.

Restart-recovery scenario kills warren mid-run; if the stub agent exits before warren reboots, the resume path becomes a no-op rather than a real test. The stub's WARREN_STUB_SLEEP_MS env knob exists for this — but the scenario must set it generously enough that warren's restart actually races with a live run.

The bwrap-in-container security flags (apparmor=unconfined, seccomp=unconfined, systempaths=unconfined, cap_add=SYS_ADMIN) are required for burrow's nested userns sandbox to come up. Container mode on macOS Docker Desktop may not honor all of these; that's why container scenarios may need a 'Linux only' skip path.

Quality gates (bun test) currently exclude the scripts/acceptance/ tree because there are no .test.ts files there. If a future code-style guard scans scripts/, biome's rules may trip on harness code that's intentionally less strict than src/.
acceptance: All 13 scenarios in scripts/acceptance/scenarios/ pass green when the harness runs in default in-proc mode against a fresh tmp dir.
scripts/acceptance/run.ts --container boots warren via docker-compose and re-runs every container-supported scenario green (or cleanly skips Linux-only scenarios on macOS).

ACCEPTANCE.md exists at the repo root and documents: (a) how to run the automated harness in both modes, (b) the --real opt-in for a real claude-code run with explicit ANTHROPIC_API_KEY guidance, (c) the fly deploy verification ritual, (d) the manual UI smoke checklist.
package.json has scripts: `acceptance` (in-proc default) and `acceptance:container` (compose-based).
warren quality gates pass after the harness lands: `bun test && bun run lint && bun run typecheck` all green.
warren-3ee3 closes via `sd close`; mulch records the harness conventions worth preserving (stub-agent contract, GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL trick, scenario file shape) via `ml record`.

sections.context: Warren has nowhere to store project-tracked, warren-specific config (cron schedules, trigger maps, per-project default role/branch/prompt). Squeezing it into .canopy/ (a prompt store) or seed extensions (per-issue metadata) is the wrong shape — and the V2 persistence rule (DB only for runtime state; definitions live in the project repo) demands a git-tracked home that travels with the project. ROADMAP R-02 proposes a new .warren/ directory in each project repo with two starter files: triggers.yaml (cron + future webhook triggers, consumed by R-06) and defaults.yaml (per-project default role/branch/prompt). This seed ships the convention, schema, loader, surface, and validation only; R-06 will consume the parsed triggers in a follow-up.
sections.approach: Add a new src/warren-config/ module that mirrors src/projects/ and src/registry/ layout (errors.ts, config.ts, schema.ts, load.ts, index.ts). Use zod for schema validation (already a dep) and pick YAML for triggers (cron expressions read better) + JSON for defaults (small, matches the rest of os-eco). Add a tiny YAML parser dep (lean toward `yaml` or `js-yaml`; decide during step 1) — both files are small and warren already has heavier deps. The loader reads <projectPath>/.warren/* lazily on demand; missing files are not errors (returns null) so existing projects keep working unchanged. Surface results via a new GET /projects/:id/warren-config endpoint (returns parsed config + per-file validation errors), render a small panel on the project detail UI, and add a doctor check that walks loaded projects and flags malformed .warren/ content. Triggers are parsed and exposed but not dispatched; R-06 picks up the parsed structure later. Defaults are parsed but only one consumer (NewRun's branch placeholder) is wired in this seed — broader consumption (defaultRole on spawn, defaultPrompt template substitution) lands in R-04/R-06 to keep this seed scoped.

alternatives.name: Stash config in .canopy/ alongside agent prompts
alternatives.rejected_because: Wrong shape — .canopy/ is a prompt store. Cron schedules and project defaults aren't prompts; mixing them collides with canopy's render contract and confuses operators reading either store.

alternatives.name: Encode trigger config as seed extensions on a sentinel issue
alternatives.rejected_because: Per-project trigger config is not per-issue metadata. Squeezing schedules into one tagged seed loses the structure and makes diffs and PR review awkward. extensions are for runtime metadata on a real issue (R-01), not for config.

alternatives.name: Store everything in warren's SQLite
alternatives.rejected_because: Violates the V2 persistence rule (DB only for runtime state). Loses git history, PR review, and travels-with-the-project. A warren VM crash should never lose a project's trigger config.

alternatives.name: TOML for both files (matches burrow.toml)
alternatives.rejected_because: Cron expressions read worse in TOML than YAML, and arrays-of-objects (the trigger shape) are noisy. ROADMAP locks YAML for triggers; defaults stays JSON for symmetry with the rest of os-eco's UI/API JSON surface.

steps.title: Pick formats + add YAML parser dependency
steps.description: Lock YAML for triggers.yaml and JSON for defaults.json (revisiting ROADMAP's 'defaults.yaml' to defaults.json given the JSON-elsewhere bias). Add the chosen YAML lib to package.json + bun.lock. Smoke-test parse + serialize roundtrip in isolation. Document the choice in src/warren-config/config.ts header comment.

steps.title: Build src/warren-config/ module (schema + loader + errors)
steps.description: Create src/warren-config/{errors.ts,schema.ts,load.ts,index.ts}. Define zod schemas matching ROADMAP R-02 sketch: triggers is an array of {id, cron, timezone?, seed, role}; defaults is {defaultRole?, defaultBranch?, defaultPrompt?}. load.ts reads <projectPath>/.warren/triggers.yaml and <projectPath>/.warren/defaults.json, returns a typed { triggers, defaults, errors } envelope where missing files yield null entries (not errors) and malformed files yield per-file ValidationError-shaped entries. Mirror the projects/ module's spawn-injectable filesystem seam so tests don't touch disk. Unit tests for: missing dir, missing single file, valid roundtrip, malformed YAML, schema violation.

steps.title: Wire loader into project lifecycle
steps.description: Call the warren-config loader on project add (cosmetic: surface validation warnings in the addProject response so operators don't silently register a project with a broken .warren/) and on each refresh. Cache the parsed result in memory keyed by project id; invalidate on refresh. Loader stays lazy — no eager reads at boot. Update src/projects/manage.ts and src/projects/refresh.ts; add tests covering cache hit/miss + invalidation.

steps.title: Add GET /projects/:id/warren-config HTTP endpoint
steps.description: Add a route in src/server/handlers.ts that returns the parsed { triggers, defaults, errors } envelope for a given project (or 404 if the project row is missing). Auth-required like other /projects routes. Update src/server/types.ts with the response shape and src/ui/src/api/types.ts + client.ts to mirror it. Tests cover: project missing → 404; project present + .warren/ absent → 200 with all-null envelope; .warren/ malformed → 200 with errors[] populated.

steps.title: Surface .warren/ config + errors in the project UI
steps.description: Add a 'Warren Config' panel on the project detail UI (src/ui/src/pages/...). Show parsed triggers (read-only table: id, cron, timezone, seed, role) and defaults (read-only key/value list). Show per-file validation errors prominently if errors[] is non-empty, with the offending path and the zod issue summary. Edit/write affordances are explicitly out of scope for this seed; the UI is read-only here. R-04/R-05 add edit surfaces.

steps.title: Add warren doctor check for .warren/ validity
steps.description: Add a new check entry in src/diagnostics/checks.ts that walks all registered projects and validates each one's .warren/ contents using the loader from step 2. Report 'ok' for projects with no .warren/ (lazy convention), 'ok' with summary counts for valid configs, and 'fail' with the path + zod issue when any project's config is malformed. Surface the new check in /readyz output and CLI doctor output (warren doctor must keep its existing seven-check ordering — append the new check). Tests cover all three states.

steps.title: Add acceptance scenario covering missing/valid/invalid .warren/
steps.description: New scripts/acceptance/scenarios/14-warren-config.ts. Provisions a project with three states in sequence: (a) no .warren/ — endpoint returns nulls, UI panel shows 'no config'; (b) valid triggers.yaml + defaults.json — endpoint returns parsed values; (c) malformed triggers.yaml — endpoint surfaces errors[]. Runs in both in-proc and container modes (per mx-1d31f0 if container-only nuance applies; otherwise default in-proc). Cleans up after itself (mx-a8d92b lookup-then-create pattern).

steps.title: Update ROADMAP + SPEC + record decisions
steps.description: Mark ROADMAP R-02 as [in-progress] then [shipped] on merge; cross-reference seed warren-571f. Add a SPEC.md note pointing to .warren/ as the per-project config home. Run ml learn + ml record decisions for: format choice (YAML triggers, JSON defaults), missing-is-not-error semantics, lazy-load contract, R-02-vs-R-06 boundary (parse here, dispatch there).

risks: Schema drift between R-02 (this seed) and R-06 (cron scheduler): if R-02's triggers schema is too narrow, R-06 has to rev it. Mitigation — bake a `kind: 'cron'` discriminator now even though only cron is implemented, so future webhook triggers can be added without breaking the schema (ROADMAP locks 'no GH triggers in this phase' but leaves room).
YAML parser dep choice may conflict with future tooling. Mitigation — use the same lib mulch/seeds use if any (check upstream); else pick the smallest, most-maintained option.

Surface area creep: the temptation to also wire defaults.defaultRole into spawn would expand this seed's blast radius. Mitigation — explicitly defer broader defaults consumption to R-04 / R-06 (capture as follow-up seeds during step 8).

Lazy-load + cache invalidation race: if two requests hit GET /projects/:id/warren-config concurrently while a refresh is mid-flight, one could see stale parsed config. Mitigation — invalidate the cache entry inside refreshProject before recordRefresh writes, and parse on demand (not eagerly), so the next request reparses.
ROADMAP item proposes defaults.yaml; this plan switches to defaults.json for symmetry. Mitigation — record the deviation in ml + update ROADMAP so future readers see one source of truth.
acceptance: src/warren-config/ module exists with schema, loader, and errors mirroring src/projects/ layout
Loader returns null entries for missing files (no error) and validation-error entries for malformed files (no throw)
GET /projects/:id/warren-config returns the parsed { triggers, defaults, errors } envelope; missing project → 404
Project detail UI renders a read-only Warren Config panel showing triggers, defaults, and per-file validation errors
warren doctor and /readyz include a .warren/ validation check covering all three states (absent, valid, malformed)
Acceptance scenario 14 passes against a live warren+burrow stack in both in-proc and container modes (or in-proc only with documented rationale)
ROADMAP R-02 status updated; SPEC.md references the new convention; mulch decisions recorded for the format and scope choices
bun test && bun run lint && bun run typecheck all pass

sections.context: Warren's only run trigger today is 'manual via UI/CLI'. Projects with recurring needs (nightly cleanup, weekly stale-issue triage, hourly docs sync) have to bolt on an external cron + webhook to talk to /runs — exactly the kind of glue warren is supposed to absorb. R-02 (pl-5d74, shipped 2026-05-10) parses .warren/triggers.yaml and exposes it via GET /projects/:id/warren-config, but no consumer dispatches the entries. Seeds v0.4.3 ships extensions.scheduledFor on issues plus `sd ready --respect-schedule` so deferred seeds stay out of the ready queue, but warren never reads the field. R-06 closes the loop: an in-process scheduler tick that walks loaded projects, fires cron entries from .warren/triggers.yaml, and dispatches one-off scheduled seeds — both as warren runs with trigger='cron' or trigger='scheduled' so the UI can label them. Both upstream prerequisites are satisfied; this seed is the warren-side wire-up plus persistence, HTTP surface, UI columns, and acceptance.
sections.approach: New module `src/triggers/` mirroring src/warren-config/ + src/runs/ layout (errors.ts, config.ts, schema.ts, repo.ts, dispatch.ts, tick.ts, index.ts). One in-process tick loop running every 60s (injectable interval) inside bootServer's lifecycle — no external daemon, no separate process, teardown via WarrenServerHandle.stop(). Per tick, for each project row: (1) call WarrenConfigCache.get() to load triggers.yaml; (2) for each cron entry, compute next-fire-at against the warren-side triggers table's last_fired_at, dispatch matches via spawnRun({trigger: 'cron'}); (3) shell out to `sd list --json` against the project clone's .seeds/, find issues with extensions.scheduledFor in the past, dispatch via spawnRun({trigger: 'scheduled'}), then `sd update --extensions` to move scheduledFor → lastScheduledRun. Persist trigger run state in a new `triggers` table (composite id `<projectId>:<triggerId>`, last_fired_at, next_fire_at, last_run_id) so the UI and next tick share one source of truth and warren restarts pick up cleanly. Use `croner` for cron parsing — small (~30 KB), tz-aware, actively maintained, no native deps; warren-config keeps the loose 5-or-6-token validation (mx-40fe51) so existing projects don't break, and dispatch falls through to croner for the strict pass at fire time. HTTP surface adds GET /projects/:id/triggers (joins parsed YAML against the triggers table for last/next fire columns) and POST /projects/:id/triggers/:triggerId/run for dry-fire. UI extends the existing read-only TriggersBlock with last/next-fire columns + a Run Now button; editing the YAML stays a git operation per the R-02 posture (mx-a5e30e).

alternatives.name: Bring-our-own cron parser (parse 5-token grammar inline)
alternatives.rejected_because: Cron grammar has enough corner cases (step values, ranges, day-of-week vs day-of-month interaction, 6-token seconds) that a homegrown parser would either be wrong or grow into croner anyway. Croner is one small dep with tz support; warren already has heavier deps (drizzle, zod, js-yaml). The 'tiny surface' argument in ROADMAP open-question #1 only holds if we skip timezone + DST handling, which we explicitly want.

alternatives.name: External cron daemon (Linux cron, systemd timers) hitting /runs via curl
alternatives.rejected_because: Pushes warren's own scheduling responsibility back onto the operator and breaks the 'one container, one HTTP API, one UI' SPEC §1 promise. Operators choosing warren explicitly want to *not* run cron. Also re-introduces auth + token-injection problems the API gate was built to avoid.

alternatives.name: Greenhouse polls GitHub + warren scheduler for cron
alternatives.rejected_because: Greenhouse is a sibling, not a subordinate (CLAUDE.md ecosystem graph). Routing cron through greenhouse would couple two repos for what is a one-process feature in warren. Greenhouse can call warren's HTTP API later for *its* trigger source (issue triage), but cron lives in warren.

alternatives.name: Catch-up missed cron runs after warren downtime
alternatives.rejected_because: Standard cron fires at time T, not 'fire N missed runs.' A 4-hour outage on an hourly trigger would dispatch 4 stacked runs and surprise operators. ROADMAP open-question #4 leans no; we follow it. Operators who want catch-up can dispatch manually via Run Now.

steps.title: Pick cron implementation + add dependency
steps.description: Decide between croner / node-cron / homegrown per ROADMAP open-question #1, install the chosen lib, and document the decision via ml. Lean croner for built-in timezone support (open-question #2) and small footprint (~30 KB, no native deps, MIT). Add to package.json + bun.lock. Confirm typecheck passes against the chosen lib's exported types. Record a mulch decision (domain: triggers) covering: lib choice, version pin, why over alternatives, and the relationship to warren-config's intentionally-loose validation (mx-40fe51) — warren-config still owns shape validation, dispatch owns grammar validation.

steps.title: Add triggers table + migration 0005 + TriggersRepo
steps.description: Schema: triggers (id TEXT PRIMARY KEY (composite `<projectId>:<triggerId>`), project_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES projects(id) ON DELETE CASCADE, last_fired_at TEXT, next_fire_at TEXT, last_run_id TEXT REFERENCES runs(id) ON DELETE SET NULL). Index on next_fire_at for the per-tick scan. Migration 0005_add_triggers_table.sql, named per the convention (mx-b4f9b5: rename drizzle output + update _journal.json). Drizzle table in src/db/schema.ts. TriggersRepo in src/db/repos/triggers.ts mirroring runs.ts/projects.ts: get(id), upsert({projectId, triggerId, lastFiredAt, nextFireAt, lastRunId}), listByProject(projectId), deleteByProject(projectId). Test suite covering FK cascade on project delete + run-id orphan-on-delete.

steps.title: Build src/triggers/ module — tick + dispatcher (cron + scheduled-for)
steps.description: New module with errors.ts (TriggerDispatchError, TriggerSeedMissingError), config.ts (WARREN_SCHEDULER_TICK_MS default 60000, WARREN_SCHEDULER_DISABLED kill switch), tick.ts (runTick({now, repos, warrenConfigs, burrowClient, spawn, logger}) — pure function, injectable everywhere), cron.ts (computeNextFireAt(cron, tz, since) + isFireDue(entry, now, lastFiredAt) using croner), seeds-extension.ts (listScheduledSeeds(projectClonePath, spawn) shells `sd list --json --label-any=...` and filters extensions.scheduledFor; clearScheduledFor(projectClonePath, seedId, spawn) shells `sd update <id> --extensions ...` to move scheduledFor → lastScheduledRun), dispatch.ts (dispatchTrigger({entry, project, source: 'cron'|'scheduled', spawnRun}) — wires the canopy role + prompt + ref into spawnRun and writes the warren triggers row before clearing the seed extension so a seed-update failure can't double-dispatch), index.ts re-exports. Per-tick algorithm: list projects → for each project, get warren-config → walk triggers.yaml entries (croner.next() vs row.lastFiredAt) → list scheduled seeds → dispatch matches sequentially (concurrency=1 inside a tick to keep ordering predictable). Skip + log when the referenced seed is closed/missing; skip + log when burrow is unreachable (probe before walking). Unit tests stub spawnRun + the seeds shell-out; integration covered later in step 7.

steps.title: Wire scheduler tick into bootServer + WarrenServerHandle.stop
steps.description: In src/server/main.ts, after the bridges + warrenConfigs construction, instantiate the scheduler with `createScheduler({deps, intervalMs: env.WARREN_SCHEDULER_TICK_MS, disabled: env.WARREN_SCHEDULER_DISABLED})`. The scheduler exposes start()/stop()/runOnce(). Default disabled=false; tests + acceptance toggle WARREN_SCHEDULER_DISABLED=1 when they don't want the loop running. Single-flight guard: if a tick is still running when the timer fires, skip + log (no overlap). stop() awaits the in-flight tick before resolving so WarrenServerHandle.stop()'s ordering (server → bridges → scheduler → burrow → db) stays clean. Add WARREN_SCHEDULER_TICK_MS + WARREN_SCHEDULER_DISABLED to the env-var inventory in src/server/config.ts.

steps.title: HTTP surface — GET /projects/:id/triggers + POST /projects/:id/triggers/:triggerId/run
steps.description: GET endpoint joins the parsed warren-config triggers list against triggers-table state — returns Array<{id, kind, cron, role, seed, timezone?, prompt?, lastFiredAt: string|null, nextFireAt: string|null, lastRunId: string|null}>. 404 if project missing; 503 (CanopyUnavailableError-style) if warren-config errors block the load. POST run-now endpoint validates the trigger id exists in the parsed YAML, calls dispatchTrigger({source: 'manual-cron'}), returns the spawned run row (201). Auth-gated like the rest of /projects/*. Add both routes to src/server/handlers.ts ROUTE_TABLE. Add to API_ROUTE_PATTERNS. Test the round-trip with a stub WarrenConfigCache + repos.

steps.title: Surface triggers + last/next-fire + Run Now in ProjectDetail UI
steps.description: Extend src/ui/src/api/types.ts with TriggerStateRow (matches the GET endpoint envelope) and runsApi.triggers.list(projectId) + runsApi.triggers.runNow(projectId, triggerId). In src/ui/src/pages/ProjectDetail.tsx replace the existing read-only TriggersBlock entries with rows that show id · cron · last fire (relative) · next fire (relative) · Run Now button. Run Now wires to the new endpoint and navigates to the new run on success. Inline text-xs feedback on errors (mx-d6ccf3). Editing remains a git operation; we never POST a YAML mutation.

steps.title: Acceptance scenario 15 — cron + scheduled-for round-trip
steps.description: scripts/acceptance/scenarios/15-triggers-dispatch.ts. Boots warren+burrow with WARREN_SCHEDULER_TICK_MS=1000 (or invokes scheduler.runOnce() via a test seam) and WARREN_SCHEDULER_DISABLED unset. Phase 1 (cron): commit a .warren/triggers.yaml with `* * * * *` referencing the stub agent + a real seed; refresh project; assert a run with trigger='cron' lands within ~5s, the triggers row populates last_fired_at, and a second tick within the same minute does NOT double-dispatch. Phase 2 (scheduled): create a seed with extensions.scheduledFor in the past (1 minute ago) and another in the future (1 hour out); refresh; assert the past one dispatches with trigger='scheduled' and its scheduledFor moves to lastScheduledRun, the future one stays untouched. Phase 3 (missing-seed): retarget the cron entry at a closed seed; refresh; assert the tick logs a skip (system event in the runs/events stream OR a structured log line) and does NOT spawn. Use the existing acceptance lifecycle helpers (mx-a8d92b lookup-then-create, mx-895738 cleanup posture). In-proc only is acceptable per scenario 14's precedent (mx-e959c0).

steps.title: Update ROADMAP + SPEC + record decisions in mulch
steps.description: Flip ROADMAP.md R-06 status to [shipped]. Add SPEC.md §13 covering: tick cadence + env vars, triggers table shape + composite-id semantics, cron-vs-scheduled trigger field, failure semantics (no catch-up, skip-on-missing-seed, skip-on-burrow-down), seeds-extension write path (scheduledFor → lastScheduledRun). Record mulch decisions (domain: triggers): cron lib + version pin (relates to step 1), no-catch-up posture, scheduledFor write-once semantics, scheduler disabled-by-default-in-tests. Update CHANGELOG.md with a 0.2.0 entry. Add R-06 follow-ups (defaults.defaultPrompt consumption — already filed as warren-af38; webhook trigger kind — future R-XX) so the next reader sees the explicit deferrals.

risks: Cron grammar lock-in: croner's grammar may differ subtly from operator expectations (Quartz dialect vs Vixie cron). Mitigation — document the exact dialect in SPEC §13 and surface croner's parse error in the GET /triggers response so operators see the failing entry without tailing logs.

Timezone confusion (ROADMAP open-question #2): per-trigger `timezone` field is in the schema today (R-02) but operators on DST-affected zones can still get bitten. Mitigation — default UTC when the field is omitted, render the per-trigger tz next to the cron expression in the UI, and document DST gotchas in SPEC §13.

Catch-up posture (ROADMAP open-question #4): operators who *want* catch-up after a warren outage will be surprised by silently-skipped fires. Mitigation — explicit docs + Run Now button covers manual replay; future signal could be an env var WARREN_SCHEDULER_CATCHUP=1, deferred to a follow-up if anyone asks.

Seeds shell-out double-dispatch: if `sd update --extensions` fails after a successful spawn, the next tick re-fires the same seed. Mitigation — write triggers.last_fired_at in warren's DB BEFORE the seed-extension clear, treat the warren row as the source of truth, and surface the failed-clear as a system event on the dispatched run so operators see the lingering scheduledFor.

Tick overrun: a slow tick (many projects × slow burrow probe) could exceed WARREN_SCHEDULER_TICK_MS and pile up. Mitigation — single-flight guard inside the scheduler (skip-if-running) so overlap is impossible; document that the worst case is reduced effective cadence, not duplicated fires.

Closed/missing referenced seed: a triggers.yaml entry pointing at a closed or deleted seed should not silently no-op. Mitigation — log a structured warning, surface it as a TriggerSkipped diagnostic on the project's GET /triggers response (per-trigger `lastSkipReason: 'seed_closed' | 'seed_missing' | …`), and consider a doctor check in a follow-up if the pattern shows up in production.

Schema drift between R-02 (warren-config) and R-06 (dispatch): if dispatch needs new fields (e.g. retry policy), warren-config will need a rev. Mitigation — keep TriggerSchema additive (all new fields optional); document the additive contract in SPEC §13.

Tests against a real `* * * * *` minute-grain cron are flaky. Mitigation — never rely on wall-clock ticks in unit tests (inject `now` + a fake interval); acceptance scenario 15 uses scheduler.runOnce() via a test seam OR drives WARREN_SCHEDULER_TICK_MS down to 1s with a tight assertion budget.

Project delete races with an in-flight tick: the tick reads project rows then walks them, but a DELETE /projects between reads could leave a stale path. Mitigation — wrap the per-project section of the tick in try/catch; FK ON DELETE CASCADE on triggers.project_id keeps the warren side consistent regardless.
acceptance: src/triggers/ module exists with errors/config/schema/repo/dispatch/tick/cron/seeds-extension/index modules mirroring src/warren-config/ layout
Migration 0005 lands, triggers table has composite-id PK + project_id FK ON DELETE CASCADE + last_run_id FK ON DELETE SET NULL, and TriggersRepo round-trips via tests
Scheduler tick is wired into bootServer + WarrenServerHandle.stop, honors WARREN_SCHEDULER_TICK_MS + WARREN_SCHEDULER_DISABLED, and has single-flight guard against overrun

Cron entries from .warren/triggers.yaml dispatch as warren runs with trigger='cron'; one-off seeds with extensions.scheduledFor dispatch with trigger='scheduled'; warren-config schema (R-02) is unchanged
After dispatch, extensions.scheduledFor is cleared (moved to lastScheduledRun) on the project's seed; failures to clear are surfaced as a system event on the run, not a hard failure
GET /projects/:id/triggers returns parsed YAML entries joined with last/next/lastRunId state; POST /projects/:id/triggers/:triggerId/run dispatches inline and returns the run row (201)
ProjectDetail UI shows last fire / next fire columns and a Run Now button per trigger; YAML editing remains git-only
Acceptance scenario 15 passes against a live in-proc warren+burrow: cron fire (no double-dispatch), scheduledFor past+future, missing-seed skip
ROADMAP R-06 flipped to [shipped]; SPEC §13 covers tick cadence, table shape, failure semantics, seeds write path; mulch decisions recorded for cron lib + no-catch-up + write-once semantics
bun test && bun run lint && bun run typecheck all pass

sections.context: Warren ships built-in `claude-code` and `sapling` agents inline (src/registry/builtins/) and burrow ships built-in `claude-code`, `sapling`, and `codex` runtimes. Pi (`@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent`) is a third coding-agent runtime distinguished by three properties warren operators care about: (1) a unified multi-provider LLM API (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Bedrock, Vertex, DeepSeek, Groq, Mistral, Azure OpenAI, custom OpenAI-compatible endpoints), (2) first-class get_session_stats RPC reporting tokens (input/output/cacheRead/cacheWrite) + cost (USD) per session, and (3) a clean customization surface — `.pi/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`, `.pi/prompts/<name>.md`, `.pi/extensions/*.ts` — that maps naturally onto warren's existing canopy-section + .canopy/agent.json seeding flow (src/runs/seed.ts). Adding pi unlocks two product surfaces: 'pick the best model per task without standing up a second runtime' and 'see what a run actually cost.' Burrow needs a piRuntime in its AgentRegistry first (currently src/runtime/registry.ts lists only claudeCodeRuntime, saplingRuntime, codexRuntime); warren's spawnRun already forwards agent.name as the burrow runtime id (mx-7352f4) and burrow's POST /burrows accepts agents: string[] (mx-c8fc41), so the warren-side surface area is the same as the sapling builtin landed via SAPLING_BUILTIN — plus four follow-on phases (customization, multi-provider, cost, event-model) that go beyond the sapling baseline. Research feeding this plan: pi RPC docs at https://github.com/earendil-works/pi/blob/main/packages/coding-agent/docs/rpc.md (commands prompt/steer/abort/get_state/get_session_stats; event kinds agent_start/end, turn_start/end, message_start/update/end, tool_execution_start/update/end, queue_update, compaction_start/end, auto_retry_start/end, extension_error); pi usage docs at https://pi.dev/docs/latest/usage (CLI flags -p, --mode json|rpc, --provider, --model, --thinking, -c/--continue, --session, --skill, --prompt-template, -e/--extension); SPEC §4.3 composition flow; burrow's AgentRuntime contract (src/runtime/runtime.ts: id, displayName, supportsResume, buildSpawnCommand, parseEvents, encodeInboxMessage, prepareWorkspace, credentialPaths, envPassthrough, installCheck).
sections.approach: Phase the work to mirror sapling's landing shape: ship a minimal pi builtin first (parity wedge), then layer the pi-specific surfaces (customization, multi-provider, cost, event-model) as separate steps with explicit forward-blocks edges. Step 0 pins the burrow-side contract via a tracked cross-repo coordination seed — without it, every subsequent warren step is blocked. Step 1 ships src/registry/builtins/pi.ts mirroring SAPLING_BUILTIN exactly (name='pi', sections.system, sections.burrow_config network='open', frontmatter.source='builtin', resolvedFrom=['builtin:pi']), wires it into BUILTIN_AGENTS, adds the builtins.test.ts row, and adds acceptance scenario 16 covering POST /runs with agentName=pi end-to-end (provisioning a burrow, seeding workspace, dispatching the run, asserting burrow.up was called with agents: ['pi']). Step 2 extends src/runs/seed.ts to materialize three new canopy section conventions — pi_skills, pi_prompts (extensions deferred, see alternatives) — into .pi/skills/<name>/SKILL.md and .pi/prompts/<name>.md inside the burrow workspace. Body format is one-skill-or-prompt-per-block via a JSON-lines envelope `{name, body}` (mirrors the expertise_seed JSONL convention already in writeExpertiseSeed), so canopy authors write the body in a single canopy section without inventing a new artifact type. Step 3 adds multi-provider surface: optional frontmatter.provider + frontmatter.model fields on AgentDefinition (registry/schema.ts), POST /runs accepts optional providerOverride + modelOverride that the spawn composer threads onto agent.frontmatter before freeze, NewRun.tsx grows two free-text Inputs ('Provider override (optional)' + 'Model override (optional)') auto-filled from the selected agent's frontmatter, and the composeDispatchPrompt path stays as-is — provider/model travel as runtime metadata on the burrow request body (new metadata.pi.provider/model fields the piRuntime reads in buildSpawnCommand). The supervisor's burrow process env passthrough widens from {ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN, ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL, CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN} to include {OPENAI_API_KEY, GEMINI_API_KEY, GOOGLE_API_KEY, PI_API_KEY, GROQ_API_KEY, MISTRAL_API_KEY, DEEPSEEK_API_KEY} — implemented as burrow's piRuntime.envPassthrough (mx-runtime contract: envPassthrough is the runtime-level escape hatch, per src/runtime/runtime.ts:97-105) so warren doesn't need to change docker-compose.yml. Step 4 adds the cost columns via migration 0006: runs.cost_usd REAL nullable, runs.tokens_input INTEGER nullable, runs.tokens_output INTEGER nullable, runs.tokens_cache_read INTEGER nullable, runs.tokens_cache_write INTEGER nullable. RunsRepo grows an `attachStats({runId, costUsd, tokensInput, tokensOutput, tokensCacheRead, tokensCacheWrite})` method (mirrors attachBurrow's partial-input semantics, mx-0a7a65). The bridge layer (src/runs/bridge.ts via bridgeRunStream) gains a pi-specific event handler that watches for agent_end / turn_end and issues a final get_session_stats RPC call against pi's stdin (when the agent is pi); on success, calls attachStats. Cost is null for non-pi runs and stays null if get_session_stats fails (best-effort; logged as a system event but does not fail the run). RunDetail.tsx adds a cost badge in the header row next to the +N-commits/PR badges (mx-6aae24, mx-f0f743) — '$0.45  •  60k/200k context' when cost_usd is set, omitted otherwise. Runs.tsx list view adds an optional sortable Cost column (hidden by default, opt-in via toolbar toggle). Step 5 widens warren's event-model UI rendering for pi-specific kinds (compaction_start/end, auto_retry_start/end, extension_error, queue_update) — these flow through events.kind verbatim today since payload_json is opaque (events table is kind: text + payload_json: json, mx-event durability), so the change is purely in src/ui/src/pages/RunDetail.tsx renderEvent() — new case branches with collapsible payload preview. Step 6 records SPEC.md updates (§4.2 list of canopy sections grows pi_skills + pi_prompts; §11 gets a new pi subsection; §3.2 'No cost or run-budget enforcement in V1' note clarifies that cost *reporting* lands here, enforcement still V2 per R-17), ROADMAP.md notes pi as a built-in alongside claude-code/sapling, mulch decisions for (a) extensions-deferred (TS-source-in-canopy-section is awkward; defer until canopy ships an artifact type), (b) MCP omission (pi explicitly has no MCP — worldview difference; R-15 stays MCP-via-canopy-frontmatter for claude-code/sapling and is not a blocker for pi), (c) provider auth posture (warren passes API keys via burrow envPassthrough; pi's OAuth /login flow is unsupported in headless mode by design — V2 reconsideration).

alternatives.name: Ship pi as an externally-installed canopy library agent, not a built-in
alternatives.rejected_because: Built-ins exist precisely so a fresh warren install can dispatch a run without CANOPY_REPO_URL set (SPEC §2.1, src/registry/builtins/index.ts:9). Claude-code and sapling both ship inline; pi is the third member of the same set and deserves the same treatment. A library-only path would mean every operator who wants pi has to stand up a canopy fork first — high friction for the headline 'multi-provider out of the box' value proposition.

alternatives.name: Use pi's --mode json (event stream) instead of --mode rpc
alternatives.rejected_because: JSON mode is unidirectional (stdout events only) and doesn't expose get_session_stats — warren would have to inflate stats from individual message_update tokens, which is error-prone and provider-specific. RPC mode is bidirectional, gives warren the exact same steer/abort/get_state/get_session_stats surface that claude-code's stream-json offers, and matches the inbox-injection seam burrow already abstracts (encodeInboxMessage). Burrow's piRuntime should pick RPC mode for the same reason it picks stream-json for claude-code.

alternatives.name: Ship pi extensions (.pi/extensions/*.ts) in this plan
alternatives.rejected_because: Pi extensions are TypeScript source modules with access to ExtensionAPI (registerTool, registerCommand). Encoding TS source inside a canopy section body works but is awkward — operators lose tsc/biome on the source, syntax errors only surface at run time, and the canopy diff view becomes useless. The right fix is a canopy artifact type (file-shaped, not section-shaped) that warren materializes alongside the rendered AgentDefinition. That's a canopy-side change first. Defer extensions to a follow-up; ship skills + prompts (which are already file-shaped + markdown) in this plan.

alternatives.name: Lump multi-provider + cost into the parity step
alternatives.rejected_because: Parity is the cheap wedge that unblocks every follow-on. Bundling multi-provider + cost into one mega-PR would (a) couple the cross-repo burrow piRuntime dependency to schema migrations + UI changes, (b) make the migration risk worse since the parity step alone has zero schema impact, (c) violate the seed's own framing ('beyond parity — separate phases'). Steps 1, 3, 4 are genuinely independent after parity lands.

alternatives.name: Track session cost via warren-side price tables instead of pi's get_session_stats
alternatives.rejected_because: Pi already reports cost.usd directly (RPC get_session_stats response: `cost: 0.45`), computed against the same models.json that drove the run. Warren maintaining a parallel price table would (a) drift, (b) require per-provider rate maintenance, (c) double the surface area of the 'add a new provider' workflow. Trust pi's number; surface it verbatim. If operators want their own cost model later, they can compute it from the tokens_* columns we persist alongside.

alternatives.name: Make pi's piRuntime supportsResume = false (treat as one-shot like codex)
alternatives.rejected_because: Pi's CLI supports -c/--continue and --session <path|id>; the underlying session store is `~/.pi/agent/sessions/` organized by working directory. The bidirectional RPC channel and persistent session file mean pi is genuinely spawn-per-turn like sapling, not one-shot like codex. supportsResume should be true; the burrow piRuntime should implement buildResumeCommand.

steps.title: Land burrow piRuntime in burrow's AgentRegistry (cross-repo coordination)
Add src/registry/builtins/pi.ts + wire into BUILTIN_AGENTS + builtins.test.ts + acceptance scenario 16 (parity smoke)
Extend src/runs/seed.ts to materialize pi_skills + pi_prompts canopy sections into .pi/skills/<name>/SKILL.md + .pi/prompts/<name>.md
Multi-provider surface — AgentDefinition.frontmatter.provider/model + POST /runs overrides + NewRun UI inputs + burrow piRuntime envPassthrough widening
Cost tracking — migration 0006 (runs.cost_usd + tokens_*) + RunsRepo.attachStats + bridge get_session_stats consumer + RunDetail cost badge + Runs cost column
Event-model widening — render compaction_start/end, auto_retry_start/end, extension_error, queue_update kinds in RunDetail renderEvent()
Docs + decision records — SPEC §pi + ROADMAP entry + mulch decisions (extensions-deferred, MCP omission, provider auth posture)

risks: Cross-repo coordination drift: warren ships its pi builtin before burrow's piRuntime lands and operators get NotFoundError('agent runtime not registered: pi') at dispatch time. Mitigation — step 0 is gated explicitly on burrow piRuntime merge; landing the warren builtin without burrow support is a hard-fail acceptance criterion. Tag both sides with a shared milestone.

get_session_stats race: pi's session-cumulative stats include the just-finished turn only after agent_end (or even after the agent process exits). Calling get_session_stats too eagerly returns stale numbers, too late risks the pi process having already exited and the stdin RPC channel being closed. Mitigation — issue the stats RPC immediately on agent_end inside the bridge (synchronous in the event handler), before the run lifecycle progresses past 'running'; treat missing/null stats as best-effort (log + nullable column) rather than a run failure.

Multi-session cost double-count: pi --continue/--session resumes a prior session, and get_session_stats returns the session-cumulative cost. Warren persists per-run cost, so a resumed run's cost would include the prior run's tokens. Mitigation — snapshot get_session_stats at run-start AND run-end via two RPC calls; persist the delta. Document this in the bridge's comment so future maintainers don't 'simplify' to a single call.

Provider env-var collision with claude-code: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is shared between pi and claude-code's CLAUDE_CODE_ENV_PASSTHROUGH. If both runtimes are active in the same burrow process, env passthrough should be a union (not an override). Mitigation — burrow's resolveSandboxEnv already unions envPassthrough sets (per runtime contract docs); confirm in scenario 16 by running pi + claude-code back-to-back against the same burrow.

Canopy section name collision: pi_skills already exists as a generic 'skills' concept (SAPLING_BUILTIN's sections include nothing called pi_skills today, but the canopy library may have authors using 'skills' generically). Mitigation — pi_skills/pi_prompts are intentionally pi-namespaced so a generic 'skills' section can coexist; document the convention in SPEC §4.2 and call it out in the canopy claude-code prompt as 'these sections are pi-specific and ignored by claude-code/sapling.'

Extensions deferred but expected: operators reading the pi docs will see .pi/extensions/ and expect warren to support it. Mitigation — call out the deferral in SPEC §pi explicitly with rationale (canopy artifact-type prerequisite), file a separate warren seed referencing this plan, link to canopy's open issue for artifact types.

MCP omission as a worldview difference: pi explicitly has no MCP support (its tool surface is built-in + extensions only). Operators expecting MCP-via-canopy-frontmatter (R-15) on pi will be surprised. Mitigation — SPEC §pi documents the omission; R-15 stays scoped to claude-code/sapling explicitly.

agent_end before tool completion (analog of mx-2fea6d for pi): claude-code is known to emit stop_reason=end_turn while a long-running bash is in flight. Pi may have an analogous shape via queue_update / auto_retry; the bridge's stats-on-agent_end handler could fire prematurely. Mitigation — observe pi's actual event order in scenario 16 (write a long-running bash through pi, assert agent_end arrives after tool_execution_end); if not, switch the stats trigger to the burrow run terminal state instead.

Migration 0006 forward-compat: adding nullable cost columns is non-breaking, but the drizzle-generated SQL must use ADD COLUMN NULL with no DEFAULT (mx-3f03bd convention). Mitigation — drizzle's auto-generation handles this correctly for nullable columns; cross-check the emitted SQL before merge.

OAuth-only providers (Anthropic Claude Pro/Max, ChatGPT Plus, GitHub Copilot via pi /login) are unsupported in headless warren: pi /login is an interactive command. Mitigation — explicitly document API-key-only posture in SPEC §pi; surface a doctor check that warns when no PI-relevant API keys are set; defer interactive-OAuth to a V2 'BYO browser' shape if anyone asks.
acceptance: src/registry/builtins/pi.ts exists with name='pi', sections.system + sections.burrow_config, frontmatter.source='builtin', resolvedFrom=['builtin:pi']; BUILTIN_AGENTS includes it; builtins.test.ts asserts schema round-trip and source provenance just like SAPLING_BUILTIN
Burrow's AgentRegistry registers piRuntime (id='pi', supportsResume=true, --mode rpc spawn shape) — confirmed via cross-repo PR link in step 0's child seed

Acceptance scenario 16 passes against a live in-proc warren+burrow stack: POST /runs with agentName='pi' returns 201 + run_xxx; burrow.up is invoked with agents: ['pi']; the run reaches state='running' and emits at least one agent_start event through warren's events table; cleanup cancels the run

AgentDefinition.frontmatter.provider + frontmatter.model land in registry/schema.ts (optional string fields); POST /runs accepts optional providerOverride + modelOverride; NewRun.tsx surfaces them as free-text Inputs auto-filled from the selected agent's frontmatter; burrow's piRuntime.envPassthrough declares the multi-provider key set

sections.context: Today warren reaches burrow exclusively through `HttpClient` (warren src/burrow-client/client.ts:35-56), which already supports both unix and TCP transports. Warren has exactly one `BurrowClient.fromEnv()` singleton (warren src/server/main.ts:71) and treats burrow as in-container. Warren SPEC §3.2 lists `No remote burrow workers` as a non-goal; that non-goal blocks self-hosting at 50+ engineer orgs (parent seed c47a in burrow as burrow-62ce), because a single warren is the concurrency ceiling for N engineers kicking off agents in parallel. This is the warren-side plan to undo that non-goal: add a `workers` table, dual `worker_id` columns on `burrows` and `runs`, a `BurrowClientPool` to replace the singleton, project-affinity placement with sticky-by-burrow semantics, fan-out reads across the live worker set, a TOML config loader so the `[workers]` block can land cleanly, worker probe + drain admin API, and a two-worker integration test. **Burrow-side surface (non-loopback bind safety, `POST /admin/drain`, OpenAPI updates, ROADMAP R-02 supersession) is tracked separately in burrow as burrow-62ce / pl-cb3e — see the burrow plan for those steps.** This plan only covers warren-side work; the joint release / version bump is owned by the burrow plan's final step.
sections.approach: Warren maps `{burrow_id → worker_id}` and routes every request to the worker that owns that burrow. The `workers` table holds `{name, url, token-shared-via-env, state}` with a three-value state machine (`healthy`, `draining`, `unreachable`); a fourth `failed` state is deferred to a future R-NN. Dual `worker_id` columns: `burrows.worker_id` is the source of truth (which worker physically holds this burrow's sandbox + SQLite row), and `runs.worker_id` is denormalized from `burrows.worker_id` at run-create time for fast lookup without an extra join. Placement (warren src/runs/placement.ts) has two rules: (A) sticky-by-burrow — if `runs.create` targets an existing burrow, the run MUST go to the worker stored on that burrow (no re-placement, no silent migration; if that worker is unreachable, fail loudly so warren can surface the error); (B) new-burrow provisioning — pick most-recent successful run's worker for the same `projectId` if its state is `healthy` (project-affinity / warm clone), else least-loaded healthy worker by in-flight run count, round-robin tiebreak by alphabetical name. Drained workers are never picked for new placement. `BurrowClientPool` (warren src/burrow-client/pool.ts) holds `Map<workerId, BurrowClient>` and exposes `placeFor({projectId})` for new burrows and `clientFor({burrowId})` for everything else; it replaces the singleton at warren src/server/main.ts:71. Zero `[workers]` block keeps today's inline-burrow behavior — the pool builds a single synthetic 'local' worker from `WARREN_BURROW_*` env vars. The `[workers]` block requires a new TOML config loader first (warren is env-only today); that loader lands as its own step and is reusable beyond this feature. Reads fan out via `Promise.allSettled` (warren src/burrow-client/fanout.ts) unioning each worker's response; per-resource reads (`burrows.get(id)`, `runs.get(id)`) and streaming use the targeted-client path via `pool.clientFor`. Worker probe loop runs every `WARREN_WORKER_PROBE_INTERVAL_MS` (default 30s) and flips state. `POST /workers/:name/drain` issues `POST /admin/drain {drain: true}` against the burrow worker (the burrow side of this is shipped by the burrow plan's `/admin/drain` step) and flips warren state to `draining`. A two-worker integration test exercises affinity, failover-on-drain, and fan-out union end-to-end. **Burrow-side wiring (non-loopback bind, `/admin/drain`, OpenAPI, ROADMAP) is tracked separately in burrow-62ce / pl-cb3e — this plan's steps 0 and 3 carry inbound cross-repo dependencies on burrow steps 0 and 3 respectively, documented in prose since cross-repo blocks aren't representable in `sd plan`.**

alternatives.name: Dynamic worker registration (workers call warren's `POST /workers` at boot)
alternatives.rejected_because: Adds a registration handshake, secret-exchange, and revocation surface that nothing in V1 actually needs. Static config is one config-file edit; workers don't move between warrens in the home-server / VPC posture. Defer until a concrete consumer (k8s operator, autoscaler) wants it.

alternatives.name: Capacity-aware / label-based placement (`gpu=true`, `cpus>=8`)
alternatives.rejected_because: No concrete operational driver in V1. Project-affinity covers the warm-clone case; round-robin covers everything else. Once labels / capacity actually matter (someone files an issue with a real workload), the placement seam built in this plan is small enough to extend.

alternatives.name: Per-worker `BURROW_API_TOKEN` via per-entry `token_env` indirection
alternatives.rejected_because: VPC-private threat model; one shared token across the pool simplifies ops (rotation = one env-var update, not N); compromise of any single worker implies network-layer breach, which is the actual threat boundary the bearer is gating. Per-worker tokens are tracked as a future hardening item (R-NN) if a concrete multi-tenancy driver emerges.

alternatives.name: Auto-migrate burrows when a worker decommissions
alternatives.rejected_because: Burrow's SQLite + sandbox state is per-host; auto-migration means rebuilding the run history, events stream, and workspace clone on the new worker. V1 mitigation is explicit operator action: `POST /workers/:name/drain` then remove from config; orphaned `burrows.worker_id` rows are surfaced by `warren doctor` as `worker_missing`. Auto-migration is V2.

alternatives.name: Lift burrow's SQLite up to warren — workers become stateless
alternatives.rejected_because: Burrow's SQLite carries run history, the events stream, sandbox metadata, and crash-recovery state. Promoting that to warren means rewriting burrow's storage layer and forfeiting per-worker crash recovery semantics. Burrow already ships durable per-worker state; the cheaper move is to teach warren which worker owns which burrow (this plan).

steps.title: [warren] `workers` table in warren SQLite + drizzle migration
steps.description: New `workers` table: `(id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT UNIQUE, url TEXT, token TEXT, state TEXT CHECK(state IN ('healthy','draining','unreachable')), last_probe_at INTEGER, created_at INTEGER)`. The `state` enum is locked to exactly three values: `healthy` (probe ok), `draining` (operator initiated drain via warren admin API; placement skips it), `unreachable` (probe failed; transient). A fourth state `failed` (probe-failure exhaustion / permanently dead worker) is deliberately deferred to a follow-up R-NN — V1 treats `unreachable` as transient and relies on operator action (remove from config) for permanent removal. Drizzle schema in warren src/db/schema.ts alongside the existing `runs.burrowId` column (warren src/db/schema.ts:93). Migration is additive — no backfill. Repo at warren src/db/repos/workers.ts with `list()`, `get(name)`, `upsertFromConfig(rows)`, `setState(name, state)`. Token column never appears in logs; redact in the repo's `toString()` debug helper if any. Cross-repo: depends on burrow-side cross-process e2e verification (burrow plan, step 0 / burrow-e2bc) — without a verified cross-process dispatcher, the warren-side worker abstraction has no contract to lean on.

steps.title: [warren] `runs.worker_id` AND `burrows.worker_id` columns + `placeFor` placement (project-affinity → round-robin, sticky-by-burrow)
steps.description: Add TWO `worker_id TEXT REFERENCES workers(id)` columns to the warren schema (warren src/db/schema.ts:93 area): one on `burrows` (the source of truth — which worker physically holds this burrow's sandbox + SQLite row) and one on `runs` (denormalized from `burrows.worker_id` at run-create time for fast lookup without an extra join). Placement function in warren src/runs/placement.ts: `placeFor({projectId, burrowId?, repos}) → workerId`. Algorithm: (A) STICKY-BY-BURROW — if `runs.create` targets an existing burrow with `burrows.worker_id` set, the run MUST go to that worker (no re-placement, no fallback; if that worker is `unreachable` the create fails with a structured error so warren can surface it rather than silently migrating). (B) NEW BURROW PROVISIONING — when there is no existing burrow, pick a worker via: (1) most-recent successful run for this `projectId` with a `worker_id` AND that worker's state is `healthy` → reuse (project-affinity / warm clone). (2) Otherwise least-loaded healthy worker by in-flight run count (round-robin tiebreak by alphabetical name). Drained workers are never picked for new placement. The chosen worker is persisted to `burrows.worker_id` BEFORE the `POST /burrows` call is issued, so a crash mid-provision leaves a recoverable row, not an orphan. `runs.worker_id` is then stamped from `burrows.worker_id` at `repos.runs.create` time. Pure function over repo reads; unit-testable with fixtures. No capacity metrics or labels in V1 — justified in the comment header (cite c47a alternatives entry).

steps.title: [warren] `BurrowClientPool`: replaces `BurrowClient.fromEnv()` singleton
steps.description: New class in warren src/burrow-client/pool.ts: `BurrowClientPool` holds a `Map<workerId, BurrowClient>`, built from the `workers` table at boot. API: `placeFor({projectId})` returns `{workerId, client}` for new burrows (delegates to placement from step 1); `clientFor({burrowId, repos})` looks up the stored `burrows.worker_id` (source of truth) — or the denormalized `runs.worker_id` when called from a run-scoped path — and returns the matching client. Falls back to a single-worker mode (env-driven `BurrowClient.fromEnv()` semantics intact) when the `workers` table is empty — this is the zero-config back-compat path. Replaces the singleton at warren src/server/main.ts:71 and every `deps.burrowClient` consumer (handlers.ts, scheduler.ts, bridges.ts — grep shows ~6 call sites). All clients share the single `BURROW_API_TOKEN`. Probe runs per-worker (`pool.probeAll()`); `/readyz` aggregates per-worker state.

steps.title: [warren] Wire `BurrowClientPool` into `spawnRun` dispatch flow
steps.description: Today warren src/runs/spawn.ts:142-170 (`provisionBurrow` + `dispatchRun`) hold one `BurrowClient` and call `client.http.burrows.up` then `client.http.runs.create`. Change `SpawnRunInput.burrowClient: BurrowClient` to `burrowClientPool: BurrowClientPool`. Before `provisionBurrow`: `const {workerId, client} = pool.placeFor({projectId: projectAfterRefresh.id})`. Persist `burrows.worker_id = workerId` BEFORE calling `client.http.burrows.up` (so a crash mid-provision leaves a recoverable row, not an orphan). Pass `workerId` through to `repos.runs.create` (now also stamps `runs.worker_id`, denormalized from `burrows.worker_id`). Use `client` for both `burrows.up` and `runs.create` — sticky to one worker for the whole dispatch, and for every subsequent `runs.create` against this burrow (per step 1 sticky-by-burrow rule). On rollback (warren src/runs/spawn.ts:236-254), `client.http.burrows.destroy` uses the same client. No change to seed.ts (it reaches through the same client). Cross-repo: depends on burrow-side `POST /admin/drain` endpoint (burrow plan, step 3 / burrow-79ad) — the drain semantics are what warren's `worker_draining` 503 surface relies on for placement to skip drained workers cleanly.

steps.title: [warren] Fan-out reads: aggregate `burrows.list` / `runs.list` / events across workers
steps.description: Today every read goes through one client (warren src/server/handlers.ts:330 etc.). Fan-out helper in warren src/burrow-client/fanout.ts: `fanoutList<T>({pool, fn, mergeKey}) → T[]` runs `fn(client)` across all healthy workers in parallel with `Promise.allSettled`, unions results sorted by `mergeKey` (createdAt), drops rejected workers with a `worker_unreachable` log line. Plumb into the handlers that today call `burrowClient.http.burrows.list` / `runs.list` / `inbox.list`. Per-resource reads (`burrows.get(id)`, `runs.get(id)`) take the targeted-client path via `pool.clientFor({burrowId})`. Streaming (events tail, run stream) is per-burrow so it just resolves the right client once and proxies — same shape as today, no fan-out needed.

steps.title: [warren] Worker probe + state transitions + `POST /workers/:name/drain` admin API
steps.description: Background probe loop (in warren's scheduler tick or a sibling): every N seconds (`WARREN_WORKER_PROBE_INTERVAL_MS`, default 30s) call `client.probe()` (warren src/burrow-client/client.ts:72) on each worker; on success set state `healthy`; on failure set `unreachable` and log. Add a warren-side `POST /workers/:name/drain` endpoint that issues `POST /admin/drain {drain: true}` against the worker (the burrow side is shipped by burrow plan step 3) and flips warren's local state to `draining` so placement skips it. Mirror with `POST /workers/:name/undrain`. Auth uses the existing warren bearer. One e2e test that drains a worker mid-dispatch and asserts placement picks the other one. Capacity metrics still out of V1.

steps.title: [warren] TOML config loader scaffolding (warren config is env-only today; add file-based loader)
steps.description: Warren's `loadServerConfigFromEnv` (warren src/server/config.ts:51) is purely env-driven — there is no TOML loader at all today. This step adds the loader infrastructure WITHOUT yet introducing the `[workers]` block (that's the next step). New module warren src/server/config-toml.ts: `loadServerConfigFromToml(path: string): Partial<ServerConfig>` parses a TOML file via `@iarna/toml` (or `smol-toml`; pick whichever already lives in warren's dep tree — neither does today, so add the smaller). Resolution order in `loadServerConfig` (renamed entry point that wraps the env loader): (1) read TOML if `WARREN_CONFIG_FILE` env var is set OR `/etc/warren/warren.toml` exists, (2) overlay env vars on top (env wins — back-compat for today's deploys). Same `ServerConfig` shape, no schema change. Test coverage in warren src/server/config-toml.test.ts: empty file → identical to env-only; populated file → values merged; env override → env wins; malformed TOML → `ValidationError` with line number. This step lands NO new config keys; it's strictly the loader plumbing so the next step can add `[workers]` cleanly.

steps.title: [warren] `[workers]` block parsing + validation + single shared `BURROW_API_TOKEN`
steps.description: Builds on the TOML loader from the previous step. Adds the `[workers]` schema: `[[workers]] name = '...' url = 'https://...'` — NO per-worker `token_env` indirection. All workers in the pool share a single token, sourced from `BURROW_API_TOKEN` (env var, never in the config file). Justification: VPC-private threat model; one shared token across the pool simplifies ops (rotation = one env-var update, not N); compromise of any single worker implies network-layer breach, which is the actual threat boundary the bearer is gating. Per-worker tokens are tracked as a future hardening item (R-NN) if a concrete multi-tenancy driver emerges. Schema validation: each entry must have a non-empty `name` (unique across the block) and a parseable `url` (https:// in prod, http:// allowed for localhost dev); `BURROW_API_TOKEN` must be set when the block is non-empty (warren refuses to start otherwise — surfaced by `warren doctor` at boot). Zero-config (no `[workers]` block) keeps today's `BurrowClient.fromEnv()` semantics: the pool is built from a single synthetic 'local' worker with `WARREN_BURROW_HOST` / `WARREN_BURROW_SOCKET` / `WARREN_BURROW_TOKEN` (warren src/burrow-client/config.ts:39-69) — the legacy single-token env var stays as the back-compat path for the inline-burrow mode. Documented in warren README + warren SPEC §10.

steps.title: [warren] Update warren SPEC: remove `no remote burrow workers` non-goal; add §5.4 multi-worker model
steps.description: Edit warren SPEC §3.2 (line 81): delete the `No remote burrow workers` bullet. Add a new §5.4 `Multi-worker model` that explains the static-config pool, `{burrow_id → worker_id}` mapping, project-affinity placement, the fan-out read semantics, and the back-compat behavior (no `[workers]` block → single inline-burrow mode unchanged). Cross-link burrow's c47a seed (burrow-62ce) + the burrow-side multi-worker doc (burrow plan step 2 / burrow-f676). Update §10.2 (Fly.io) with a multi-warren-machine sketch. No `[workers]` block is the canonical home-server posture; the multi-worker config is the org-of-50 posture.

steps.title: [warren] Two-worker integration test: project-affinity + failover + cross-worker fan-out
steps.description: tests/multi-worker.test.ts: spawn two `burrow serve` subprocesses on distinct TCP ports, point a warren test instance at both via `[workers]` config, dispatch two runs for the same `projectId` and assert they land on the same worker (affinity). Mark worker A as draining via `POST /workers/A/drain`; dispatch a new run and assert it lands on B. List runs via warren's API and assert fan-out unions both workers' state. Tear down. This is the load-bearing test that proves the whole stack works end-to-end. Uses the burrow-side cross-process e2e harness (burrow plan step 0 / burrow-e2bc) to confirm dispatch actually fires on each worker. This step is the warren-side gate for the joint release (burrow plan, final release step).

risks: Stale `worker_id` after a worker is decommissioned: burrows on that worker become unreachable. V1 mitigation: explicit operator action (`POST /workers/:name/drain` then remove from config); orphaned `burrows.worker_id` rows are surfaced by `warren doctor` as `worker_missing`. Auto-migration of burrows across workers is V2.

Project-affinity stickiness can pile load on one worker for a hot project. Mitigation: placement only honors affinity if the worker is `healthy` AND not draining; if affinity is unhealthy, fall through to round-robin. Real capacity-aware placement is deferred (alternatives entry).

Network partition between warren and a worker mid-dispatch leaves a half-provisioned burrow. Mitigation: warren's existing rollback path (warren src/runs/spawn.ts:236-254) already handles `BurrowUnreachableError`; the pool's `clientFor` retries through the transport-mapping wrapper so transient blips bubble up as structured errors.

Fan-out aggregation hides per-worker errors. Mitigation: `Promise.allSettled` + structured `worker_unreachable` log for rejected workers; `/readyz` includes per-worker probe state so operators can spot a degraded pool.

Sticky-by-burrow with an unreachable worker fails loudly rather than migrating. This is a feature (no silent split-brain), but operators need to know to drain + remove a dead worker. Mitigation: `warren doctor` reports orphaned burrows; failure mode is a structured error surfaced to the warren API caller, not a hang.

TOML config loader is new infrastructure landing alongside its first consumer (`[workers]`). Mitigation: step 6 lands the loader in isolation with its own test coverage so the `[workers]` step (step 7) builds on a tested foundation.

Probe loop runs unconditionally even in zero-config mode (single synthetic worker). Mitigation: probe of the local worker is cheap; if it ever becomes a hot path, gate behind `pool.size > 1` (tracked as a follow-up if observed).
